title: The Resonance Decay Archive
hypothesis: Each record emits a unique tone that degrades with repeated access. Long-press protects a record but paradoxically weakens neighbors. Sound becomes the primary indicator of archival health—users navigate by ear, not eye.
dataModel: Seeded audio fragment records with pitch integrity values (0.0-1.0), each containing text fragments, accession dates, and harmonicity scores. Variable count based on seed (7-31 records).
recordCountStrategy: Seeded from current hour + date hash. Formula: 7 + (seed % 24) yields range 7-31. Each cycle produces different counts.
interactionModel: mousedown/touchstart long-press (800ms+) locks a record's pitch, incrementing protection but decreasing adjacent records' harmonicity by 0.15. click plays current tone—each playback decreases pitch integrity by 0.08. pointermove across records creates sympathetic resonance restoring nearby degraded tones by +0.03. State persists in sessionStorage; tab closure causes total loss.
stateModel: Each record: {id, textFragment, accessionDate, pitchHz, harmonicity, protectionCount, playCount, pitchIntegrity, neighbors[]}. Pitch integrity affects tone quality and visual opacity. sessionStorage stores full state. Protection increases visual weight but decreases neighbor harmonicity.
visualLanguage: Muted earth tones (ochre, slate, faded indigo). DOM cards with visible text fragments. Opacity and border degradation reflect pitch integrity. Protected records gain heavy borders while neighbors visually fray. No glow, no particles, no radial effects. Typography-driven with monospace accession IDs and serif fragments.
expectedStructuralChange: Introduces audio-primary archival health indicator. Users must listen to assess record condition. Long-press protection creates strategic tension between saving one record and damaging others. sessionStorage enforces impermanence—the archive exists only while the session lives. Moves from visual grid toward multi-sensory experience where sound degradation IS the archival damage.
